text: The surgeon enters the lesser sac and uses a vessel sealer to divide the short gastric vessels, visibly separating them from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses a Penrose drain to retract the liver, providing clear access to the surgical field for the pancreatectomy procedure.
A 3-0 Vicryl suture is placed on the posterior stomach, which is then externally retracted to expose the splenic hilum.
The surgeon dissects the posterior aspect of the stomach, revealing the underlying anatomical structures and facilitating further exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon focuses on the inferior aspect of the pancreas and spleen, preparing for further dissection or manipulation in the surgical field.
The surgeon divides the splenocleidomastoid ligament, defines and dissects the inferior border of the pancreas, and divides the mental branch from the splenic artery.
Following the division of the omentum, the surgeon places an additional suture on the posterior stomach to enhance exposure of the pancreatic neck, facilitating further dissection.
The surgeon uses Mehron bipolar forceps to expose the middle colic vein, directing it towards the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon uses a specialized instrument to carefully dissect small branches of the pancreas, ensuring precision and safety during the procedure.
The surgeon dissects the superior portion of the pancreas, followed by the removal of hepatic artery lymph nodes from the common hepatic artery.
The surgeon tunnels through the pancreas and divides it using a surgical stapler, completing the separation of the pancreatic tissue.
The surgeon carefully dissects and ligates the dorsal pancreatic artery at the root of the splenic artery, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon encircles the splenic artery and applies a clamp to secure it, preparing for further manipulation or ligation.
Fluorescent angiography is used to confirm the preservation of blood flow in the hepatic artery, with the artery visibly highlighted by the fluorescent dye.
The surgeon clips and divides the splenic artery, completing the separation of this vessel from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon divides the splenic vein using a surgical stapler, completing the separation of the vein from surrounding structures.

The surgeon dissects the deeper layer of the retroperitoneum behind the splenic artery, revealing fibrosis. The dissection exposes the aorta and the left adrenal vein, allowing for sampling of the retroperitoneal lymph node at this location.
The surgeon completes the dissection by following the retropaternium fascial plane, ensuring clear separation of the tissue layers in the surgical field.
The surgeon places a 19 French Blake drain at the conclusion of the pancreatectomy procedure.
